request: We want to notify the right people when a freelancer's mission is about to end.

problem.value: Avoid missions ending without renewal, replacement, or final invoicing being anticipated — prevent gaps in freelancer coverage and revenue leakage.
problem.evidence: Request frames the need as 'notify the right people when a mission is about to end' — implies a current lack of visibility on upcoming mission ends.

current_process.evidence: Not stated whether this is tracked today manually (spreadsheet, calendar reminder) or not at all.

success_metrics.evidence: No stated measure (e.g., % missions renewed/closed on time, reduction in last-minute scrambles).

actors.value: Likely candidates: the freelancer's manager/internal owner, the Client contact (operational or HR role), possibly the freelancer themselves.
actors.evidence: Context: 'Client/Contact — notification routing frequently depends on the contact's role, not just the client.' Request only says 'the right people', undefined.

business_objects.value: Mission (start/end date), Freelancer, Client, Contact(s) with roles.
business_objects.evidence: Mission and Freelancer explicitly named; Client/Contact inferred from context as the entities carrying the recipients.

business_rules.value: Undefined: how many days/weeks before end date counts as 'about to end', and whether this threshold varies by client, contract type, or mission length.
business_rules.evidence: Context: 'a business rule can vary by client, contract or country' — a recurring trap; not addressed in request.

workflow.value: Undefined: is this a scheduled/async check (daily job scanning upcoming end dates) firing a notification event, and how it reacts to mission extension/early termination after a notice was already sent.
workflow.evidence: Context: 'End-of-mission is an event that can trigger notifications, final invoicing and validation' — but the described need is *before* end, a distinct trigger from end-of-mission itself, not covered in request.

integrations.value: Channel (email/in-app/both) and whether this should also feed into other flows (e.g., pre-triggering final invoicing or a renewal task) not specified.
integrations.evidence: 'notify' implies specifying who, when, and which channel — named recurring trap in context; raises default impact from medium to high.

permissions.value: Undefined who can configure notification rules/recipients vs. who merely receives them; also whether client-side contacts should see internal mission data.
permissions.evidence: Context: permissions are frequently forgotten until acceptance testing, and are shared/cross-cutting in this platform.

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear if notice period / recipient list is a global default or configurable per client/contract.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Platform is explicitly configurable multi-client; 'client-specific: approval circuits, business rules, notification routing' per context card.

constraints.evidence: No deadline or dependency mentioned yet.

edge_cases.value: Mission extended after notice sent (should it recompute/cancel the alert?), mission ended early, multiple missions ending same week, contact missing/incomplete data.
edge_cases.evidence: Context traps: idempotency (don't notify twice), incomplete source data, retroactive changes.

reporting.value: Possibly a simple list/dashboard of upcoming mission ends; no compliance driver named.
reporting.evidence: No audit/regulatory language in the request — kept at baseline low impact.

risks.value: Risk of duplicate notifications, or of missed notifications when mission dates are edited after the fact.
risks.evidence: Context trap: idempotency and synchronous/asynchronous trigger design apply directly to a scheduled notification job.

questions.q: Who exactly are the 'right people' to notify — the internal manager/owner of the mission, the Client contact (and which role: operational, HR, billing?), the freelancer themselves, or some combination?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: high/high — notification routing in this platform depends on contact role, not just the client; getting this wrong misdirects a potentially sensitive alert.

questions.q: What defines 'about to end' — a fixed lead time (e.g. 15/30 days before end date), and does that threshold vary by client, contract type, or mission length?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: high/high — this rule is the crux of the feature; if it varies by client it needs configuration UI, not a hardcoded constant.

questions.q: Should this run as a scheduled/asynchronous check (e.g. nightly scan of end dates), and what happens if the mission is extended or ended early after a notice was already sent?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: high/high — sync vs async and recompute-on-change are different builds; unaddressed, this becomes the classic 'notify twice or not at all' bug.

questions.q: Should the notice period and recipient list be configurable per client/contract, or is one standard rule fine for all missions?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: high/high — platform is multi-client configurable; this decides whether we build a settings screen now or hardcode and retrofit later.

questions.q: Which channel should the notification use (email, in-app, both), and should it also kick off any other action (e.g. prompting final invoicing or a renewal decision)?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: high/high — 'notify' is never just a channel choice; if it should trigger a downstream flow, that changes scope significantly.

summary.objective: Automatically alert the right stakeholders ahead of a freelance mission's end date, so renewal, replacement, or closing actions (final invoicing, offboarding) aren't left to chance.
summary.scope: Likely includes: a scheduled check comparing today's date against each active Mission's end date, a configurable lead-time rule (possibly per client/contract), a recipient-resolution step (internal manager plus the relevant Client Contact, matched by role), and a notification dispatch (email and/or in-app) — with logic to avoid duplicate sends and to react to mission date changes after a notice has already gone out.
assumptions: Recipients include at least an internal owner/manager and a Client Contact, likely selected by role (operational/HR), not just 'the client' generically.
The 'about to end' threshold is a lead time before the mission end date, potentially configurable rather than hardcoded.
The check runs as an asynchronous scheduled job rather than a synchronous, inline trigger.
Notification channel is email and/or in-app, consistent with existing platform notification patterns.
No regulatory/audit obligation is attached to this specific notification (kept reporting impact low pending confirmation).
summary.blind_spot: The request assumes 'notify' is a single simple action, but it hides at least three separate decisions: who counts as 'the right people' (role-based routing, not just the client), what precise rule defines 'about to end' (and whether it varies by client/contract), and how the system behaves when a mission's end date changes after a notice was already sent (risk of duplicate or missed notifications). Skipping these turns a one-line request into a fragile, silently wrong automation.

problem.value: Avoid missed hand-offs at mission end: give client/HR/freelancer time to decide on renewal, plan final invoicing, and organize offboarding before the mission actually ends.
problem.evidence: 'notify the right people when a mission is about to end' — implies an anticipatory action, not a post-hoc log.

current_process.evidence: Not stated — likely manual tracking (spreadsheet or memory) today, to confirm.

actors.value: Likely: the freelancer's manager/operational contact, the Client's operational or HR contact, and possibly the freelancer themself. Exact set unconfirmed.
actors.evidence: Context: 'Client/Contact ... notification routing frequently depends on the contact's role, not just the client.'

business_objects.value: Mission (has start/end date), Freelancer, Client, Contact.
business_objects.evidence: Request names 'mission' and 'freelancer' explicitly; Client/Contact are structurally linked per context.

business_rules.value: Lead time before end (e.g. 30/15/7 days) and whether it varies by client, contract type, or mission length — unspecified.
business_rules.evidence: Context recurring trap: 'a business rule can vary by client, contract or country.'

workflow.value: Likely a scheduled check comparing today's date to mission end date, firing at one or more thresholds; may need to fire again if mission end date is changed (extension).
workflow.evidence: Context: 'End-of-mission is an event that can trigger notifications, final invoicing and validation.'

integrations.value: Channel unspecified — email, in-app notification, or both.
integrations.evidence: Recurring trap: '"notify" implies specifying who, when, and which channel.'

permissions.value: Who can configure the lead-time rule, who can see the upcoming-end list, and whether the freelancer sees it themselves are all undefined.
permissions.evidence: Context: 'permissions are forgotten until acceptance testing.'

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear whether notification lead-time/routing is a single global rule or configurable per client.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'Client-specific: approval circuits, business rules ... notification routing.'

constraints.evidence: No deadline or regulatory driver named.

edge_cases.value: Mission extended after notification sent, mission cancelled early, freelancer has multiple concurrent missions, contact has left the client.
edge_cases.evidence: Context: end-of-mission events interact with extension/renewal scenarios.

reporting.evidence: No compliance/audit driver named for this specific feature.

risks.evidence: Not defined yet — e.g. duplicate notifications, missed notifications if end date changes.

questions.q: Who exactly must be notified — the freelancer, the client's operational/HR contact (and which role specifically), the internal manager, or several of these? Does it depend on the contact's role at the client?
questions.why: high impact × empty: 'the right people' is the crux of the request but is not defined — wrong recipients defeats the whole feature; also determines the permission model.

questions.q: How far in advance should the alert fire (e.g. 30 days, 15 days, multiple reminders), and does this lead time vary by client, contract type, or mission length?
questions.why: high impact × empty: the timing rule is the core business logic; if it varies by client it needs to be configurable, not hardcoded — this changes the build significantly.

questions.q: Should this rule/routing be configurable per client (like other notification routing in the platform), or is one global rule acceptable for all clients initially?
questions.why: high impact × empty: platform convention makes routing client-specific by default — building it hardcoded now likely means rework later.

questions.q: What happens if the mission's end date changes after a notification has already been sent (extension, early cancellation) — should it re-trigger, cancel a pending alert, or be ignored?
questions.why: high impact × medium uncertainty: end-date changes are common and not handling them risks stale or duplicate notifications — a recurring trap in this domain (idempotency).

questions.q: Which channel(s) should carry the notification — email, in-app, both — and is this informational only, or should it also let someone act (e.g. trigger a renewal/extension workflow) directly from it?
questions.why: medium-high impact × empty: channel and actionability change the UI/integration scope substantially and are never implied by 'notify.'

summary.objective: Automatically alert the relevant people ahead of a freelance mission's end date, so renewal, offboarding, and final invoicing can be handled before the mission lapses.
summary.scope: Likely: a scheduled check on Mission end dates, a configurable lead-time rule, notification dispatch to one or more roles (freelancer, client contact, internal manager) via email/in-app, with logic to handle date changes (extension/cancellation) to avoid duplicate or missed alerts.
assumptions: The goal is proactive planning (renewal/offboarding/final invoicing), not just record-keeping.
Recipients likely include a client-side contact and/or an internal manager, in addition to or instead of the freelancer.
The lead-time rule may need to vary by client/contract, consistent with other business rules in this platform.
Notification should account for mission end-date changes (extension, early cancellation) to remain accurate.
summary.blind_spot: The request names no recipient, no lead time, and no channel — 'the right people' is exactly the ambiguity that decides whether this is a one-line scheduled job or a configurable, per-client notification-routing feature with edge-case handling for changed end dates.

problem.value: Avoid missed end-of-mission actions: renewal/extension decisions, offboarding, final invoicing, coverage gaps.
problem.evidence: Not stated explicitly; inferred from domain notes ('end-of-mission is an event that can trigger notifications, final invoicing and validation').

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned — unknown whether this is currently manual, tracked in a spreadsheet, or absent entirely.

success_metrics.evidence: Not stated — no measure given for what 'working' means (e.g., zero missed renewals, faster final invoicing).

actors.value: Likely candidates: mission manager, client contact (operational role), HR/admin, freelancer themselves — but not specified.
actors.evidence: 'the right people' is the crux of the request and is left undefined; context stresses notification routing depends on contact role, not just client.

business_objects.value: Mission entity (linked to Freelancer and Client) with a start and end date; end-of-mission is a known event type.
business_objects.evidence: From product context: 'Mission ... linked to a Freelancer and a Client, has a start and an end.'

business_rules.value: Undefined: how long before end counts as 'about to end', and whether this lead time varies by client, contract type, or mission length.
business_rules.evidence: Not stated; context flags that business rules commonly vary by client/contract — a recurring trap.

workflow.value: Undefined: is this a scheduled check (async, e.g. daily job scanning missions) or an event fired at a fixed offset before end date? Single alert or repeated/escalating reminders? What if the end date changes after a notification was sent?
workflow.evidence: Not stated; context explicitly flags sync-vs-async trigger design and idempotency as recurring traps for 'notify on event' requests.

integrations.value: Undefined: notification channel (email, in-app, SMS), and whether this should also trigger downstream actions (extension workflow, draft final invoice).
integrations.evidence: Context: '"notify" implies specifying who, when, and which channel' — none of these three are given.

permissions.value: Visibility of mission end-date info likely follows existing role/contact-role restrictions, but not confirmed.
permissions.evidence: Inferred from context: 'permissions are forgotten until acceptance testing' and contact-role-based routing.

config_vs_custom.value: Likely client-specific (lead time, recipients, channel) rather than a single hardcoded rule, per platform configurability model.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'Client-specific: approval circuits, business rules, ... notification routing' — but not confirmed for this feature.

constraints.evidence: No deadline, budget or dependency mentioned.

edge_cases.value: Unaddressed: mission extended/cancelled after alert fires, missing end date, multiple concurrent missions per freelancer, duplicate notifications.
edge_cases.evidence: Not discussed; standard risk zone per context's idempotency and incomplete-data traps.

reporting.evidence: No compliance/audit obligation named for this specific feature; not worth probing yet.

acceptance.evidence: Not defined yet — depends on resolving actors, timing rule and channel first.

risks.value: Risk of notification fatigue (too many/duplicate alerts) or missed alerts if end date changes; rollout risk if recipients list is wrong at launch.
risks.evidence: Inferred from domain traps around idempotency and one-to-many notification routing.

questions.q: Who exactly should be notified — the mission's internal manager, the client contact (and which role: billing/operational/HR), the freelancer, or a combination — and does this vary by client or contract?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: 'the right people' is the core ask and is completely undefined; recipient routing in this platform depends on contact role, not just client, so this changes the whole notification design.

questions.q: What counts as 'about to end' — a fixed lead time (e.g. 15/30 days before end date), and can that lead time differ by client, contract type, or mission duration?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: timing threshold is unstated and the context flags that such rules commonly vary by client/contract — this directly determines the trigger logic and configurability needs.

questions.q: Should this run as a scheduled/batch check (e.g. nightly job scanning upcoming end dates) or fire from a specific event, and should it repeat/escalate if no action is taken? What happens if the mission's end date is later changed or the mission is cancelled after a notification was sent?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: sync-vs-async trigger design and idempotency are named recurring traps for exactly this kind of 'notify before X happens' request; picking wrong means rework of the whole trigger mechanism.

questions.q: Which channel should the notification use (email, in-app, both), and should it also kick off any downstream action — e.g. prompting a mission extension decision or drafting the final invoice?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: channel and downstream linkage are unspecified; if this is meant to feed extension/invoicing workflows the scope is much larger than a standalone alert.

questions.q: Is the lead time, recipient list and channel meant to be configurable per client/contract by administrators, or is a single global rule acceptable for now?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: platform is multi-client and notification routing is normally client-specific configuration — building it hardcoded vs configurable is a major effort difference decided upfront.

summary.objective: Automatically alert the right people before a freelancer's mission ends, so renewal, offboarding, and final-invoicing decisions happen on time instead of being missed.
summary.scope: Likely involves: a scheduled or event-based check against Mission end dates, a configurable lead-time rule, resolution of recipients (mission owner, client contact by role, and/or freelancer), a notification send (channel TBD), and safeguards against duplicate/missed alerts when dates change.
assumptions: The trigger is a lead-time-before-end check rather than a same-day event (inferred from 'about to end').
Recipients likely include an internal mission manager and a client contact with an operational/HR role, not just 'the client' generically.
Notification rules (lead time, recipients, channel) are probably meant to be configurable per client/contract, consistent with the platform's existing configurability model.
This is a net-new feature with no existing manual process to preserve or migrate.
summary.blind_spot: The request names no timing rule, no explicit recipient list, and no behavior for when a mission's end date changes or the mission is cancelled after a reminder has already gone out — without these, the team could build a rigid, hardcoded, single-notification alert that immediately breaks under real client variation or a rescheduled mission.
